title: Superradiant LIDAR
authors: T. Kullick, M. Bojer, J. von Zanthier, G. S. Agarwal
abstract: In recent years, light detection and ranging (LIDAR) has seen a steep rise in the sensitivity of measuring the distances of remote objects. Here, we propose to enhance the sensitivity of LIDAR even further by exploiting Dicke's concept of superradiance, i.e., the collective light emission of statistically independent light sources. By using $N$ thermal light sources (TLS) and measuring intensity correlations of order $m \geq 2$ instead of $m=1$, i.e., the intensity, we show that the Cramér-Rao bound on the measurement of the distance of a remote object undercuts that of traditional LIDAR by a factor of $N$, and can be reduced further with increasing correlation order $m$. Our numerical calculations are supported by analytical expressions for the special cases of two and three TLS and a general approximate expression for any number of TLS.
summary: The paper proposes a new LIDAR technique called Superradiant LIDAR that uses multiple independent thermal light sources and higher-order intensity correlations. This method significantly improves distance measurement sensitivity, outperforming traditional LIDAR by a factor proportional to the number of light sources used.
main_problem: Enhancing the sensitivity of Light Detection and Ranging (LIDAR) for measuring the distance of remote objects beyond the limits of traditional and coherent LIDAR.
main_result: The proposed Superradiant LIDAR achieves a Cramér-Rao bound that is undercut by a factor of N compared to traditional LIDAR, with sensitivity further improved by increasing the correlation order m.
method: The authors use analytical derivations and numerical evaluations of the m-th order photon correlation function and Fisher information, developing a high-precision fit function to describe the scaling behavior.
model_or_system: A system of N statistically independent thermal light sources (TLS) placed equidistantly along an axis, where light is reflected by a remote object and detected using m-th order intensity correlations.
key_observables: m-th order correlation function (G(m)_N) and Fisher information (F_{z2}^{(sup)})
important_parameters: Number of light sources (N), correlation order (m), unknown distance (z2), and phase differences (delta_i)
main_assumptions: Measurements follow Poisson statistics, light sources are identical and uncorrelated, and pixel width is small enough for the phase approximation to hold.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental schematic; Figure 2 compares correlation functions and shows the steeper slopes for higher N; Figure 3 highlights the significant gains in Fisher information for larger N and m; Figure 4 compares the accuracy of the analytical lower bound versus the proposed fit function.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the LIDAR problem, proposes the Superradiant LIDAR scheme, provides the mathematical framework for correlation functions, derives analytical bounds and fits for Fisher information, and validates these with numerical simulations.
why_interesting: It demonstrates how exploiting collective effects (superradiance) and higher-order correlations can significantly enhance parameter estimation precision, which is a core theme in quantum metrology and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Sketch of a practical implementation of Superradiant LI- DAR. N equidistant TLS emit light that is separated by a beam split- ter. The transmitted light is detected by m −1 detectors at a known distance. The reflected light propagates towards a remote object. After being reflected by the remote object, the light travels towards the mth detector. Correlating the photons of all m detectors yields a measurement of the mth-order correlation function G(m) N (δ1, δ2), from which the unknown distance to the remote object can be extracted. Note that the measurement is insensitive to atmospheric turbulence.

Figure 2. (a) Conceptual scheme of Superradiant LIDAR. N equidistantly spaced, identical, statistically independent TLS are placed at positions Rl, l ∈{1, . . . , N}, along the x-axis. The radiated light is detected by m detectors at positions r j, j ∈{1, . . . , m}, in the far field. The mth detector is placed at an unknown distance z2 under a variable angle ϑ2 ≈x2/z2, whereas the remaining m −1 detectors are placed at a distance z1 under the angle ϑ1 ≈x1/z1. (b) The mth-order correlation function of N TLS of the Superradiant LIDAR setup ˜G(m) N (δ1, δ2) is shown for the cases N = m = 2 (purple) and N = m = 10 (blue), highlighting that higher-order correlation functions of N > 2 light...

Figure 3. Fisher information F (sup) z2 of Superradiant LIDAR as a func- tion of the correlation order m ∈{2, 3, . . . , 20} and the number of TLS N ∈{2, 3, . . . , 20}. The special case N = m = 2 (green rectangle), equivalent to two-photon LIDAR, marks the minimum of F (sup) z2 .
Figure 1. Detailed proposal for a Superradiant LIDAR setup. For details, see text. GGD: rotating ground-glass disk, BS: beam splitter, CCD: charge-coupled device camera.

Figure 2. The relative difference ∆Frel between the numerically evaluated Fisher information of Superradiant LIDAR (Eq. (C7)) and the approximated Fisher information in Eq. (D5) displayed for N ∈{4, 5, . . . , 20} and m ∈{2, 3, . . . , 20}. The cases N = 2, 3 are not shown, as analytical solutions are presented in Eqs. (6) and (7) in the main text, respectively. All deviations are below 9 %, and the approximate form always underestimates the numerical results.
